notes: Winnow and pick the dry mix apart into seed you can actually sow on purpose.
Winnow and pick the dry mix apart into seed you can actually sow on purpose.
Hard-hammer percussion to rough the wedge, then grind the edge on coarse stone.
Hard-hammer percussion to rough the wedge, then grind the edge on coarse stone.
Strip the bark and whittle the stick straight and smooth with a fresh flake edge.
Slurry dirt with water in the bucket, let it settle, and screen the heavy clay fraction through plant fibre. Wet, dirty, low-yield -- but it works anywhere with dirt and a water source.
Slurry dirt with water in the bucket, let it settle, and screen the heavy clay fraction through plant fibre. Wet, dirty, low-yield -- but it works anywhere with dirt and a water source.
Render trimmed fat low and slow, skim the clean tallow off the cracklings, and let it set hard.
Soak the stalks until the woody core rots free, then beat and comb out the long bast -- the line fibre for cloth and packing.
Weave the line fibre tight on a warped frame; close, even cloth that holds wind without tearing.
Pound a spare fired brick to grit; mixed back into raw clay it stops the shrink-cracks that ruin a pot.
Screen river sand to an even grit -- the sand option when no fired ceramic is spare to crush for grog.
Bore a hardwood block to the shaft and pack it with tallow; it runs cool enough for slow mill work.
Split a billet and whittle a handful of tapered pegs; driven dry they swell into the hole and lock.
Stand split staves in a ring and bind them with a tight rope hoop; swelling wood seals the seams.
Lay a sealed brick chamber with a condensing offtake; the wood gas is driven through it instead of up a vent.
Bake a sealed charge so it carbonises without burning; the offtake condenses the wood tar the clamp lets escape.
Boil tar down until the light fractions cook off and it sets stiff -- pitch that seals seams hard instead of staying tacky.
Score the bark, peel it off in slabs, and curl the inner bark into strips -- the log is now clean stock.
Score and split a stripped log lengthwise into rough flat planks. Slow without a metal saw, but the planks unlock shovel scoops and cooperage.
Knap a core to a gouging lip set across the long axis, then grind it keen for transverse cuts.
Knap a core to a gouging lip set across the long axis, then grind it keen for transverse cuts.
Flake a flat, broad blade and grind one long edge; it lashes broad-side to the haft.
Seat the bit transverse on a knee-bend haft and lash it tight; the edge faces the worker.
Lash the blade across the haft at a chopping-down angle for turning soil.
Dish a rough board into a scoop, split the haft to grip its neck, and lash it fast.
Bore a dense billet, drive the haft through, and bind the cheeks so it cannot split out.
Whittle a hardwood billet to a clean taper; season it hard so it bites instead of crushing.
Strip a stout pole and flatten one end into a prying tip; the longer the pole the more it lifts.
Hollow a shallow batea from a wood round; the gentle dish lets heavy fines settle while you swirl off the light sand.
Set a narrow flake end-on in a split stick and bind it so a mallet blow drives straight through.
Lash a long flake between two short grips so it can be pulled toward you to shave bark and round stock.
Bend a green stave into a bow and string it tight; the cutting blade is fitted later when metal exists.
Loop the cord once around a straight spindle so sawing the bow spins it -- drills holes and starts fire.
Weight a spindle with a holed stone whorl so a flick of the cords keeps it turning through the bore.
Tie even knots along a stretched rope as repeatable length marks; wind it on a stick to carry.
Melt the bronze over charcoal and pour the axe bit into the stone mold; knock it out cool and grind the edge.
Melt the bronze over charcoal and pour the axe bit into the stone mold; knock it out cool and grind the edge.
Pour the heavier twin-point pick head; more metal than an axe bit, so it takes a longer, hotter melt.
Pour a broad dished blade; planish the face cool so it sheds soil instead of holding it.
Seat the bit in the haft's eye and lash it; a cast axe holds an edge far longer than a knapped one.
Wedge the pick head onto the haft and bind it; unlocks mining of oxide and sulfide ores.
Boil the charge until pressure builds and tap one puff into a holding flask. Water goes in the input, charcoal in the fuel slot.
The dynamo's commutator and brushes rectify the armature's generated AC into smoothed DC. One steam charge (one engine cycle) drives one output charge.
The dynamo's commutator and brushes rectify the armature's generated AC into smoothed DC. One steam charge (one engine cycle) drives one output charge.
Slip rings instead of a commutator let the alternating current through unchanged. One steam charge per output cycle at single-phase workshop voltage.
Three-phase windings at 120° offset triple the power of a single-phase machine from the same prime-mover input. The three-phase output powers large AC induction motors and district distribution.

Mercury arc rectifier: AC strikes an arc at the mercury pool cathode; only forward-biased half-cycles conduct. The arc voltage drop and mercury vapour loss consume ~33% of the input power -- two DC charges out for every three AC charges in.

Iron-core transformer steps voltage up for long-distance transmission, doubling the effective charge throughput per cycle at the cost of matching step-down at the far end. AC-only -- DC has no transformer equivalent.
Square up a dry log, bed it on stones and lash it tight into a heavy chopping block.
Square up a dry log, bed it on stones and lash it tight into a heavy chopping block.
Hang and break down a cow carcass into quarters; trim steaks, salvage hide, fat, bones, horns and sinew.
Dress a sheep, peel the pelt with fleece intact and trim usable mutton, fat, bone, sinew and hooves.
Field dress and quarter a deer; save loins, haunches, hide, antlers, leg sinew and hooves.
Pluck, eviscerate and joint a small game bird, keeping feathers, down, giblets and a little fat.
Skin, gut and joint a rabbit -- two lean cuts, a small hide, and bones.
Quick skin and dress; barely a snack of meat but the hide is clean.
Dress a raccoon for dark, gamey meat and a decent light hide.
Pluck and dress an owl -- large flight feathers are the primary yield; little meat.
Carefully skin a fox to keep the pelt intact, then dress for lean, dark meat.
Break down a wolf for its thick pelt, tough lean meat, large bones, and a fang.
Breaking down a bear is heavy, slow work -- but the fat yield alone makes it worthwhile.
Field dress a boar for pork belly, lard fat, thick hide, tusks and hooves.
Skewer beef on a stick and sear over hot coals until the outside chars and the inside is cooked through.
Thread mutton onto a stick; the fat renders as it roasts, basting the meat from within.
Venison is so lean it cooks quickly; watch it closely or the outside dries before the inside is done.
Game bird breast on a stick over the coals; white meat cooks fast and turns firm and pale.
Rabbit is delicate and very lean; cook gently or it toughens. Eat with rendered fat if you have it.
Tiny portion; cooks in minutes. More symbolic than sustaining, but every calorie counts.
Dark gamey meat; cook it through completely -- raccoons carry parasites. The fat renders nicely.
Fox meat has a sharp, distinctive flavour. Lean and tough if rushed; rest it after the heat.
Wolf meat is intensely flavoured and very tough. Long slow heat over coals is the only way.
Never eat bear raw -- always cook it thoroughly. The fat renders into the meat as it cooks.
Wild boar pork is much fattier than domestic pig; the fat spits and smokes as it cooks.
Camel is lean and dark; sear it over the coals until just cooked through.
Gamey and tough -- cook it long and thoroughly to make it safe and palatable.
A small, lean cut; it cooks fast over a flame -- a quick desert meal.
Dark and oily; the fat renders heavily as it cooks, making it intensely calorie-dense.
Firm, mild meat; cook it through for a hearty meal on the shore.
Sweet shellfish meat; cook gently over the coals until firm and opaque.
Skewer a chunk of red meat on a stick and roast over the campfire.
Thread white meat onto a stick and roast quickly for a simple cooked skewer.
Slowly melt and strain mixed animal fat over the campfire into clean rendered tallow.
Crack and pound roasted bones into a coarse bone meal for farming or glue work.
Spread fresh blood thin on trays and sun-dry into dense, storable blood meal.
Soak and work a cow hide in bark liquor, then stretch and dry into heavy tanned leather.
Dress, soak and smoke a deer hide into softer, lighter leather for clothing and lacing.
Wash and work a sheep pelt so the fleece stays on while the hide cures into a flexible backing.
Simmer small bones and hide offcuts into a thick collagen stock that sets as raw gelatin.

worldSources: Gather an armful of dead branches from trees and shrubs around your camp.
Rake together scattered sticks from the forest floor into a small pile.
Pick up fine twigs from shrubs and small branches knocked down by wind.
Collect a mix of twig sizes from the general forest litter around camp.
Pick up individual small, fully dried sticks from open ground and brush.
Select especially thin, dry twigs from sheltered spots for use as kindling.
Rake together loose leaves lying on the forest floor into a thin layer.
Lift up dense, packed leaf mats that have settled and compressed over years.
Peel up cohesive moss mats from shaded rocks, logs, or stumps.
Pick small individual moss tufts from damp stone or tree bases.
Scoop up the fresh, light surface layer of fallen conifer needles.
Dig into deep, older needle layers under big conifers for thicker bedding.
Collect whole cones dropped beneath conifer trees.
Break apart dry cones to collect loose cone scales from the forest floor.
Find scattered nut shell fragments under feeding trees and old middens.
Hand-pick or sift dry seeds from spent flower heads and litter around camp.
Cut fresh small trunks or limbs into short green logs.
Cut or break fully dried small stems from deadfall into short logs.
Split or cut thicker dead trunks into medium-length firewood logs.
Harvest generic construction wood from felled trees and large branches.
Cut tall grasses or reeds and tie them into a compact thatch bundle.
Strip inner bark or fibrous stems from suitable plants into long fibers.
Cut tall reeds along the edges of lakes, ponds, or marshy ground.
Harvest flexible willow wands from riverside trees and shrubs.
Pry off thick bark slabs from fallen logs or storm-damaged trunks.
Peel long bark strips from suitable trees when the sap is running.
Dig loose mineral dirt from shallow pits or exposed banks.
Cut clods of dark, spongy humus from beneath the leaf and needle mat.
Scoop wet mud from puddles, stream banks, or the edge of ponds.
Scoop loose sand from river bars, lake shores, or sandy patches.
Shovel or scoop loose snow from open ground or drifts in cold weather.
Cut or chip blocks of ice from frozen ponds, streams, or packed snow.
Pick up rough fieldstones from the soil surface or shallow digs.
Collect smooth, rounded stones from shallow riverbeds and gravel bars.
Dig sticky clay from riverbanks, pond margins, or subsoil below the topsoil.
Break dark, fine-grained basalt from exposed volcanic outcrops or boulders.
Chip pale, coarse-grained granite from exposed bedrock or large stones.
Break pale limestone from exposed cliffs, quarry faces, or eroded slopes.
Collect discrete nodules of limestone rock from talus piles or outcrops.
Chip banded or veined marble from rare metamorphic rock outcrops.
Break friable sandstone blocks from layered cliff faces or escarpments.
Split thin plates of slate from fissile dark rock beds.
Chip rock salt from crusted deposits, dry lake beds, or shallow caves.
Chip bituminous coal from dark seams exposed in cliffs or stream cuts.
Pick up generic ore-bearing rocks from mineral-stained patches or scree.
Chip copper-stained ore rocks from green or blue veined outcrops.
Break heavy reddish-brown iron ore rocks from exposed seams or boulders.
Collect pale tin ore rocks from hard rock veins or coarse gravel bars.
Harvest clearly marked safe berry bushes along forest edges and clearings.
Gather nuts from marked safe trees or from groundfall beneath them.
Collect generic wild berries from bushes and undergrowth (less curated mix).
Strip seed heads from patches of wild grasses and grain-like plants.
Skin hunted animals to recover raw hides before any tanning or curing.
Select a comfortable, rounded cobble from riverbeds or gravel bars to use as a hammerstone.

notes: Bundled fine twigs snapped down into a single dry stick.
Bundled fine twigs snapped down into a single dry stick.
Snap a dry stick down with loose twigs into quick field kindling.

Soak inner-bark strips in fresh water (retting) until the bast fibers separate from the woody cortex; rinse and tease them apart. Works any time you have bark on hand -- no biome dependency. The bucket comes back empty.
Tease moss and dry leaves into a fluffy tinder bundle.
Rake loose leaves into a thicker packed mat.
Press moss tufts together into a moss mat.
Bind two handfuls of fine forest twigs around a pair of dry sticks into a compact branch bundle.
Pick through mixed berries into one safe edible batch.
Pack ripe blueberries into a safe edible batch.
Pack ripe blackberries into a safe edible batch.
Sort hawthorn haws into a safe edible batch.
Mash tart sea buckthorn into a safe edible batch.
Strip juniper berries; safe in small batches.
Heat-treat rowan berries to drive off raw-toxicity into safe food.
Crack and shell hazelnuts into a safe edible batch.
Sort mixed forest nuts into a safe edible batch.
Leach and crack acorns into safe food -- raw acorns are tannin-bitter; this is the slow path.
Winnow forest seed mix down into a small handful of usable grain.
Twist three clumps of plant fibres into a rough rope.
Pick the best hammerstone out of a couple of rounded river stones.
Fallback: pick a usable hammerstone from two ordinary stones.
Cleave a smooth river stone into rougher chunks. Useful when a recipe wants 'stone' rather than the polished river kind.
Commit a natural hammerstone as your first basic tool.
Bind a branch bundle with rope and anchor it with two river stones. Plain fieldstone works the same way if you have it instead.
Bundle three fallen branch chunks into a useful armful of material.
Lash two dry spindle sticks together with rope; the flake notches the hearth board. Use at any fire ring to start a fire.
Scrape and cut a deer hide into strips; they shrink into rawhide cord.
The thick cattle hide yields more rawhide cord than lighter hides.
Scrape and cut a light hide into narrow rawhide strips.
Thin small-game hide scraped into a single length of rawhide cord.
Thick hide from large game yields two lengths of heavy rawhide cord.
Score and split a fresh oak log into usable wood billets.
Birch splits cleanly along straight grain into flat billets.
Pine splits easily but leaves resin on the flake edge.
Dense maple requires several strikes to split cleanly.
Beech is hard work to split but the resulting billets are strong.
Straight-grained ash splits with satisfying ease.
Split an unidentified hardwood log into rough billets.
Split a generic green log into rough construction billets.
Pack three handfuls of dirt into a solid earth block. Place with the shovel to build small walls or dam channels.
Tie a rag-and-stick figure on a stake. Animals won't approach crops within its shadow.
Twist plant fibres into a clean strip. Tie over a wound to stop bleeding and heal.
Crush leaves into a base of rendered fat. Eases scrapes and small burns.
Braid two lengths of rope together and weight the loop end with a rawhide knot.
Weave flexible willow wands into a lidded basket; plant-fiber binding keeps it shut.
Lash timber billets into a drop-gate cage sturdy enough for deer or foxes.
Tie a chunk of cooked meat to a rope stake; the blood-scent draws wolves from downwind.
Wrap plant fibres around the grip of a raw hammerstone to seat it comfortably in the hand.
Wrap plant fibres around the grip of a raw hammerstone to seat it comfortably in the hand.
Strike a rounded cobble with a hammerstone to knock off several sharp flakes.
Notch a stick and lash a sharp flake into place with twisted plant fibres.
Shape and grind a stone blank, then lash it to a branch handle for a proper camp axe.
Tease and twist plant fibres into fluffy tinder bundles that catch fire easily.
Break or shave dry sticks into smaller, fast-lighting kindling pieces.
Arrange smooth cobbles into a ring that keeps coals and sparks contained.
Lay tinder, kindling and dry logs inside a stone ring to form a reusable hearth.
Wrap a dry stick with fibre and pine resin, warming it just enough to soak in.
Score, bend and lash bark strips into a watertight bark bucket with a simple handle.
Dip the bucket into a nearby stream, pond or spring to carry water back to camp.
Bring water to a rolling boil (1 min at low altitude; 3 min at high altitude).
Heat stones in the fire, then drop them in to keep water near-boil for the required time.
Cut hide into strips and dry them on a rack until they shrink into strong rawhide cord.
Fold and stitch a softened hide into a crude waterskin, laced with rawhide cord.
Lean and lash branch bundles into a simple A-frame that can accept debris cover.
Layer bundles of reeds and grass over the frame to shed rain and hold warmth.
Align reed stems and tie them into a tight thatch bundle.
Pack moss mats and lash them together -- softer than reed thatch, slower to dry but warmer.
Layer packed leaf mats and tie them into a soft, slightly leaky thatch bundle. Use when reeds and moss are scarce.
Pack dry thatch and fibres into a simple roll that insulates you from the ground.
Wrap a straw core in hide and stitch it into a roll that can survive daily use.
Trim a hide and punch holes so it can be tied as a rough weather cloak.
Prop poles into a rack and lash them so split logs can dry off the ground.
Excavate a shallow pit and line it with stone to keep food cool and off wet soil.
Scrape a shallow hollow and ring it with branches as the camp's waste dump.
Lash bark panels between sapling uprights; the simplest camp storage box (27 slots).
Peg and lash split wood into a proper chest with a lid; weatherproof storage (27 slots).
Lash uprights and crossbars into a rack that can hold meat, hides and cordage.
Weave flexible willow rods and lash them into a panel that walls pits or breaks wind.
Dig a deeper cache and line/cover it with wattle for longer-term storage.
Stack and cover a wood pile with dirt to form a simple earth-mound charcoal clamp.

Era 2 (~5-day real burn). Smoulder covered wood in the clamp to carbonize it into charcoal (~33% yield). Wood gas + tar vent and disperse (not captured at clamp scale); ash collects at the chimney base -- saved as potash / lye.
Wedge and shape clay into a small pot (greenware).
Hand-build a larger clay form; yields two greenware pots ready for kiln firing.
Build a simple updraft kiln from clay, earth, and stone.
Fire clay into durable bricks using charcoal heat.
Fire greenware pots into durable earthenware.
Grind grain on a saddle quern into coarse meal.
Grind grain longer for finer flour on the saddle quern.
Strip, reverse-twist and braid plant fibres into a single length of rope.
Shape a loop of rawhide cord and stake it between small sticks as a trail snare.
Notch two dry sticks together for the rod, lash plant-fiber line along the length, and fix a sharpened knife flake as the hook. Roughly 20 catches before it wears out.
Weave flexible rods into a funnelled trap that fish can swim into but not out.
Warm safe berries and nuts at the hearth into a more filling, digestible meal.
Peck a depression into a stone block to make a mortar for grinding grain or pigments.
Pick and trim a stone that fits the hand for pounding and grinding in the mortar.
Grind and smooth a stone into a saddle-shaped quern for more efficient milling.
Flatten logs with a ground axe and lash them into a sturdy raised work surface.
Peck a groove around a stone head and lash it tightly to a stick handle with rope.
Split a large bone flat and lash it to a stick as a crude scoop.
Rough field dressing of a cow carcass. Messy but provides meat, hide, bone and fat. Use a Butchering Block for 3× the yield.
Field dress a sheep for mutton, light hide, and a small bone.
Field dress a deer for venison, supple hide, and a structural bone.
Pluck and gut a game bird. Small yield but quick.
Skin and gut a rabbit. Quick work for a small but useful yield.
A tiny carcass. Yields a small bite of meat and little else.
Field dress a raccoon for dark meat, a light hide, and some fat.
Pluck large owl feathers carefully before gutting for white meat.
Skin the fox carefully to preserve the quality pelt, then gut for lean meat.
A wolf is hard work to field dress. Pelt, meat, and a solid structural bone.
Enormous effort. Bear yields exceptional fat, rich dark meat, and a thick hide.
Field dress a wild boar. Watch the tusks -- and expect a lot of fat.
Air-dry an oak log on the rack. Dense wood takes time.
Birch dries quickly on the rack due to its low water content.
Resinous pine needs to dry so it doesn't spit when burned.
Dense maple takes patience to dry fully.
Beech is the densest hardwood; allow extra drying time.
Ash seasons faster than most hardwoods.
Season an unknown hardwood log on the rack.
Resinous spruce dries faster than most hardwoods.
Larch seasons more slowly than spruce but has better rot resistance.
High-resin alpine fir takes longer to season but produces excellent fuel.
Soft willow dries quickly; poor fuel but good for carving and weaving.
Dry alder for fuel or keep green if it will be used in water.
Stretch and dry a deer hide on the rack to produce supple, workable hide.
Cow hides are large; they yield two usable hide pieces after drying.
Thin small-game hide dries quickly on the rack.
Fine rabbit or bird hide dries very quickly.

Unlike sulfide ores, hematite (Fe₂O₃) is already fully oxidized -- almost no mass is lost on roasting. The purpose is to drive off moisture and crack the ore structure for better reduction in the bloomery. Yield is effectively 100%.

Unlike sulfide ores, hematite (Fe₂O₃) is already fully oxidized -- almost no mass is lost on roasting. The purpose is to drive off moisture and crack the ore structure for better reduction in the bloomery. Yield is effectively 100%.
Bog iron (limonite, FeO·OH) is roasted to dehydrate it to hematite/magnetite before bloomery reduction. Loses ~10% mass as water (2FeO·OH → Fe₂O₃ + H₂O). Low hardness means it roasts quickly.
Era 3 (~30min hand work). Break roasted ore to walnut-fist size -- too large makes cold spots, too fine chokes airflow. Reject white silica veins. Crushing dust scatters; no byproduct worth a slot.
Carbon monoxide from charcoal reduces FeO·OH to spongy metalite iron at ~1100-1200°C. The resulting bloom contains trapped slag and must be hammered to consolidate.
Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂. Hematite is harder and needs longer reduction time than bog iron, but produces a cleaner bloom with less sulfur.

Era 3 (~45min active, 3-5 reheats). Hammer the hot bloom at welding heat from the centre outward -- welds the iron crystals and squeezes slag to the surface, leaving a dense but unsized billet. ~10-15% comes off as hammer scale (iron oxide); squeezed slag joins the heap. A cold bloom shatters -- reheat between passes. Draw the billet to bar next.

Era 3 (~20min). Reheat and hammer the billet with overlapping blows, rotating to draw it down to an even, measurable cross-section. Surface scale here is incidental -- no byproduct; collect pigment from the consolidation step instead.
Native copper is elemental metal -- no chemical reduction needed. Heat to ~1085°C in a clay crucible to melt and pour into a stone mold.
SnO₂ + 2C → Sn + 2CO₂. Carbon reduction at ~1100°C is required before the metallic tin can be tapped -- its low melting point of 232°C only applies after reduction.
Controlled 9:1 Cu:Sn melt gives ~10% tin bronze -- the historically documented sweet spot. Below ~6% Sn gives little hardness gain; above ~17% the alloy becomes brittle.

Smelting copper ore and cassiterite together without pre-measuring ingots produces an unpredictable tin fraction. Yield is lower than controlled alloying: 5 native copper + 2 cassiterite + 8 charcoal → 3 bronze here. The controlled route (same ore mass) smelts separately → 1 copper_ingot + 0.5 tin_ingot → alloy → 3 bronze AND you know the exact ~10% Sn. The Bronze Age switched to controlled alloying for consistency, not just yield.
Stamp mill crushes lump hematite to a fine fraction suitable for blast furnace burden or wet separation. ~25% losses to tailings.
Crushed magnetite can be magnetically separated at higher tiers; for now it serves as a direct blast furnace feed.
Bloomery and furnace slag retains trapped iron particles. Crushing and re-feeding recovers a useful fraction -- important when ore is scarce.
Crushed limestone (CaCO₃) is the flux for blast furnaces. Fines that pass through the screen partially calcine during crushing heat, yielding a quicklime byproduct (CaO).

The blast furnace drives a continuous reduction column: coke burns to CO at tuyere level (~1600 degC), CO reduces Fe2O3 to iron as the burden descends, and limestone flux (CaO after calcination) reacts with gangue silica to form liquid slag (CaSiO3) that floats above liquid pig iron. Pig iron absorbs ~4% C from coke -- it is hard but brittle. The slag must be tapped separately from the iron notch.

The blast furnace drives a continuous reduction column: coke burns to CO at tuyere level (~1600 degC), CO reduces Fe2O3 to iron as the burden descends, and limestone flux (CaO after calcination) reacts with gangue silica to form liquid slag (CaSiO3) that floats above liquid pig iron. Pig iron absorbs ~4% C from coke -- it is hard but brittle. The slag must be tapped separately from the iron notch.

The Bessemer blow forces cold air through liquid pig iron from tuyeres at the converter base. Oxygen in the air burns out carbon, silicon, and manganese in a violent 15-20 minute exothermic reaction -- the heat released is so intense that no external fuel is needed after ignition. Carbon drops from ~4% to ~0.2-0.5%, producing steel. The blow must be stopped at the right carbon level; over-blowing produces wrought iron, under-blowing leaves cast iron.
Steel ingots are passed between hardened rolls under full steam pressure. The rolling action reduces cross-section, work-hardens the surface, and aligns the grain for better tensile properties.
Steel ingots are passed between hardened rolls under full steam pressure. The rolling action reduces cross-section, work-hardens the surface, and aligns the grain for better tensile properties.
Hot pig iron rolled through a profiled rail die. The I-section profile distributes wheel load across the foot and head; air-cooling after rolling relieves thermal stress.
Universal H-beam section rolled from steel billets. Structurally superior to wrought-iron box girders for the same mass -- flanges carry bending load, web carries shear.

Regenerative open-hearth process: incoming cold gas heats through a brick checker-work that was itself heated by the last exhaust stroke. The reversal cycle maintains furnace temperature while using far less fuel than a non-regenerative flame. Operator controls carbon content precisely -- Bessemer is faster but open-hearth produces more consistent structural steel grades.
Quartz silica is melted in the lampworking burner flame and drawn by hand into tube stock. Borosilicate composition (with trace mineral impurities) tolerates rapid thermal cycling without cracking.

Glass tube is collapsed over the filament mount, the air evacuated, and the stem sealed with a gas flame. Using pre-drawn tube stock is faster than blowing a bulb from scratch and gives consistent wall thickness.
Coarse stamp-crush to liberate chalcopyrite from gangue. ~1 in 6 reports to fines as stone dust. Muscle/trip-hammer -- pay in time, not energy.
Fine grind to ~75 micron flotation size. Over-grind sheds a little more stone dust.
Condition with lime (depress pyrite, pH ~9-12), add a xanthate collector + pine-oil frother; copper sulfide floats off, silicate gangue sinks as tailings. 4 ground -> 3 concentrate.
Partial roast: 2CuFeS2 + O2 -> Cu2S + 2FeS + SO2. Burns off part of the sulfur (SO2 captured for the acid plant) and starts oxidizing iron. Mass drops with the lost sulfur.
Matte smelt with silica flux. Overall: 2CuFeS2 + 2SiO2 + 4O2 -> Cu2S + 2FeSiO3 + 3SO2. Iron leaves as iron-silicate (fayalite) slag; copper + sulfur collect as matte (Cu2S.FeS, ~45-60% Cu).
Peirce-Smith two-blow. Slag blow: 2FeS + 3O2 -> 2FeO + 2SO2, then 2FeO + SiO2 -> fayalite slag. Copper blow: Cu2S + O2 -> 2Cu + SO2. Taps blister copper, ~98-99.5% Cu.
Anode-furnace fire refining: oxidize off S/Fe/Pb/Zn, then "pole" with green wood/charcoal to reduce Cu2O back to metal -- tough-pitch copper (~99%). Drops a copper-rich refining slag (recycled).
Cast blister/fire-refined copper into flat anodes for the electrorefining tankhouse.
Electrolytic refining in CuSO4/H2SO4. Anode: Cu -> Cu2+ + 2e-; cathode: Cu2+ + 2e- -> Cu (99.99%). Au/Ag/Se/Te drop as anode slime.
Remelt cathode under charcoal cover and cast oxygen-free refined ingot for wire and electronics.
Hot-roll copper ingot to plate stock.
Cold-roll plate down to thin foil.
Roll/forge ingot to round rod stock.
File/grind ingot to copper powder for alloying and powder metallurgy.

Dead-roast pyrite in excess air: 4 FeS2 + 11 O2 -> 2 Fe2O3 + 8 SO2. Drives off the SO2 acid feed AND leaves an iron-oxide cinder -- the same roasted hematite the iron chain smelts. One of the oldest acid feedstocks; the other is captured copper-smelter SO2.

Catalytic oxidation over vanadium(V) oxide beds: 2 SO2 + O2 <=> 2 SO3 (dH=-196 kJ/mol), ~450C and 1-2 atm. Exothermic + reversible, so heat is pulled between beds; double-contact/double-absorption (DCDA) pushes ~98%+ conversion and keeps SO2 stack emissions low.

Absorb SO3 into circulating strong acid: SO3 + H2SO4 -> H2S2O7 (oleum). SO3 is never fed to water directly -- the hydration is so violent it forms a fog of acid mist that will not condense. Make-up water keeps the loop charged.
Bleed oleum down with a metered water add: H2S2O7 + H2O -> 2 H2SO4. Yields concentrated ~98% acid; part is drawn off as product, part recycled back to the absorption tower.

The pre-Contact route (Roebuck, 1746): SO2 oxidised by moist air with recycled nitrogen oxides (NOx) as catalyst inside lead-lined chambers. Cheap and robust but caps out near 65-78% acid. NOx is recovered in the Gay-Lussac/Glover towers and reused.
Boil chamber acid up in the Glover tower to drive off water and lift it toward concentrated strength. Tier-3 path to H2SO4 before the vanadium contact plant is available.

worldSources: surface water: lake, river, rain barrel, or hand-dug well
notes: Scoop raw, untreated water from any surface source. Gathered, not crafted -- the base input for acid dilution, slurries, quench, and cooling. Boil/filter downstream for potable or process-grade water.
